news_summary: Recent advances in health highlight innovative biomaterials for tissue healing, enhanced nutritional value in strawberries, cutting-edge quantum technology for cancer cell analysis, and remarkable cases of cancer remission without treatment.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées récentes dans le domaine de la santé mettent en avant des biomatériaux innovants pour la cicatrisation des tissus, une valeur nutritionnelle améliorée des fraises, une technologie quantique de pointe pour l'analyse des cellules cancéreuses, ainsi que des cas remarquables de rémission du cancer sans traitement.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances en el ámbito de la salud destacan los biomateriales innovadores para la cicatrización de tejidos, el aumento del valor nutricional de las fresas, la tecnología cuántica de vanguardia para el análisis de células cancerosas y casos notables de remisión del cáncer sin tratamiento.

articles.title: Breakthrough biomaterial heals tissue from the inside out
articles.summary: Scientists have developed a breakthrough injectable biomaterial that travels through the bloodstream to repair damaged tissue from within, reducing inflammation and jumpstarting healing. In animal studies, it successfully treated heart attack damage and even showed promise for conditions like traumatic brain injury and pulmonary hypertension. Unlike earlier approaches that required direct injection into the heart, this new therapy can be delivered intravenously, allowing it to spread evenly and act quickly.
articles.content:
A biomaterial designed to travel through the bloodstream could offer a less invasive way to calm inflammation and help injured tissue repair itself. In animal studies, the injectable material improved tissue damage caused by heart attacks in both rodents and large animals. Early proof of concept experiments also suggested that the same approach may one day be useful for other inflammation driven conditions, including traumatic brain injury and pulmonary arterial hypertension.

"This biomaterial allows for treating damaged tissue from the inside out," said Karen Christman, a professor of bioengineering at the University of California San Diego, and the lead researcher on the team that developed the material. "It's a new approach to regenerative engineering."

The findings were reported in Nature Biomedical Engineering in 2022 by a team of bioengineers and physicians. At the time, Christman said a human study testing the safety and effectiveness of the biomaterial could begin within one to two years.

A New Route for Repairing Heart Damage

Heart attacks remain one of the most serious medical emergencies in the United States, with an estimated 785,000 new cases each year. When blood flow to the heart is blocked, cardiac tissue can be injured or die. The body responds by forming scar tissue, but that scar does not contract like healthy heart muscle. Over time, this can weaken the heart and contribute to congestive heart failure.

There is currently no established therapy that directly repairs heart tissue after a heart attack. Existing care focuses on restoring blood flow, limiting further injury, and managing the risk of future heart problems.

"Coronary artery disease, acute myocardial infarction, and congestive heart failure continue to be the most burdensome public health problems affecting our society today," said Dr. Ryan R. Reeves, a physician in the UC San Diego Division of Cardiovascular Medicine. "As an interventional cardiologist, who treats patients with coronary artery disease and congestive heart failure on a daily basis, I would love to have another therapy to improve patient outcomes and reduce debilitating symptoms."

From Heart Hydrogel to Bloodstream Infusion

The work builds on earlier research from Christman's team involving a hydrogel made from the natural scaffolding of cardiac muscle tissue, also known as the extracellular matrix (ECM). That gel was designed to be delivered directly into damaged heart muscle through a catheter. Once in place, it forms a supportive structure that encourages cell growth and tissue repair.

Results from a successful phase 1 human clinical trial of that earlier hydrogel approach were reported in fall 2019. The trial found that transendocardial injection of VentriGel, a cardiac extracellular matrix hydrogel, was safe and feasible in post heart attack patients with left ventricular dysfunction, although larger randomized studies would be needed to test whether it improves outcomes.

The direct injection method, however, has an important limitation. Because it requires a needle based injection into the heart muscle, it generally cannot be used immediately after a heart attack. Delivering it too soon could risk additional injury.

That challenge pushed the researchers toward a different idea: a biomaterial that could be infused into a blood vessel in the heart during procedures such as angioplasty or stenting, or delivered through an IV.

"We sought to design a biomaterial therapy that could be delivered to difficult-to-access organs and tissues, and we came up with the method to take advantage of the bloodstream -- the vessels that already supply blood to these organs and tissues," said Martin Spang, the paper's first author, who earned his Ph.D. in Christman's group in the Shu Chien-Gene Lay Department of Bioengineering.

Why IV Delivery Matters

The bloodstream based approach gives the biomaterial a major practical advantage. Instead of staying in a few injection sites, it can spread more evenly through damaged tissue. That could make it especially valuable after a heart attack, when injured areas may be difficult to reach directly and time is critical.

The Nature Biomedical Engineering study described the material as an intravascularly infused extracellular matrix biomaterial made from decellularized, enzymatically digested, and fractionated ventricular myocardium. The material was designed to localize to injured tissue by binding to leaky microvasculature and was largely degraded within about three days.

How the Biomaterial Is Made

To create the injectable version, researchers in Christman's lab began with the hydrogel they had already developed and tested for compatibility with blood injections. The problem was particle size. The original hydrogel contained particles that were too large to effectively target damaged, leaky blood vessels.

Spang solved this by processing the liquid precursor of the hydrogel in a centrifuge. This allowed the team to separate out larger particles and keep only nano sized particles. The material was then dialyzed, sterile filtered, and freeze dried. When sterile water is added to the final powder, it becomes a biomaterial that can be delivered intravenously or infused into a coronary artery in the heart.

How It Finds Injured Tissue

When the researchers tested the biomaterial in a rodent model of heart attack, they expected it to move through leaky blood vessels and into damaged tissue. After a heart attack, gaps can form between endothelial cells, which line the inside of blood vessels.

Instead, the team saw something more surprising. The biomaterial attached to those endothelial cells, helped close the gaps, and appeared to speed up blood vessel healing. That process reduced inflammation, one of the major drivers of tissue damage after injury.

The researchers then tested the treatment in a porcine model of heart attack and saw similar results. In rats and pigs with induced acute myocardial infarction followed by intracoronary infusion, the biomaterial was linked with reduced left ventricular volumes, improved wall motion scores, and gene expression changes associated with tissue repair and inflammation.

Potential Beyond the Heart

Although most of the work centered on heart attack damage, the researchers also tested whether the same biomaterial could target other inflamed tissues. In rat models, they found proof of concept that the approach could be useful for traumatic brain injury and pulmonary arterial hypertension.

That broader potential is one of the most intriguing parts of the work. Many organs and tissues are difficult to access directly, but all are supplied by blood vessels. If a biomaterial can use those vessels as a delivery route, regenerative medicine may be able to reach injuries that are otherwise hard to treat.

"While the majority of work in this study involved the heart, the possibilities of treating other difficult-to-access organs and tissues can open up the field of biomaterials/tissue engineering into treating new diseases," Spang said.

What Has Happened Since the 2022 Study

Since the original study, related work has continued to explore how extracellular matrix based biomaterials influence repair after myocardial infarction. A 2025 Nature Communications study from researchers including Christman used spatial transcriptomics and single nucleus RNA sequencing to examine how injectable extracellular matrix biomaterials affect heart tissue after myocardial infarction. The study found pro repair signals involving immune modulation, blood vessel and lymphatic development, fibroblast activation, myocardial salvage, smooth muscle cell proliferation, and neurogenesis in rat models.

That later work did not replace the need for clinical testing of the intravascular biomaterial, but it added more detail about how this class of cardiac extracellular matrix therapies may influence healing at the cellular and regional level inside injured hearts.

Ventrix Bio, Inc., the startup cofounded by Christman, has also continued advancing related cardiac extracellular matrix technology. A ClinicalTrials.gov listing for VentriGel describes a phase 1 open label study in children with hypoplastic left heart syndrome, sponsored by Emory University, to assess safety and feasibility of intramyocardial injection of the Ventrix Bio extracellular matrix material. The listing was not yet recruiting when accessed.

Next Steps for Human Testing

Christman and Ventrix Bio have planned to seek FDA authorization to study the newer intravascular biomaterial for heart conditions in humans. If cleared for clinical testing, the therapy would need to show that it is safe, practical to deliver, and effective enough to improve patient outcomes.

For now, the treatment remains experimental. But its appeal is clear: rather than requiring direct injections into heart muscle, it could potentially be delivered through existing blood vessel based procedures or by IV, reaching injured tissue from within.

"One major reason we treat severe coronary artery disease and myocardial infarction is to prevent left ventricular dysfunction and progression to congestive heart failure," said Dr. Reeves. "This easy-to-administer therapy has the potential to play a significant role in our treatment approach."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant biomedical breakthrough: an injectable biomaterial that can be delivered intravenously to repair damaged heart tissue and reduce inflammation, with potential applications for other serious conditions. This innovation offers a less invasive, more effective treatment for heart attack damage, a major public health issue, and shows promise for broader regenerative medicine applications. The article provides detailed context on the development, testing in animal models, and potential for human clinical trials, demonstrating substantial real-world impact and significance.
articles.personality_title: New injectable material helps heal damaged heart tissue from inside the body
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Heart attacks cause serious damage to heart muscle, and current treatments focus on restoring blood flow but cannot repair the injured tissue itself. Scientists have been looking for ways to help the heart heal and reduce harmful inflammation after such injuries.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of California San Diego developed a new injectable biomaterial that can travel through the bloodstream and reach damaged heart tissue. Tested in rats and pigs, this material attaches to injured blood vessels, helps close gaps, reduces inflammation, and encourages tissue repair. Unlike earlier treatments that needed direct injections into the heart, this biomaterial can be given through an IV or during common heart procedures.

**Impact** – This new method is less invasive and can spread evenly through damaged tissue, making it easier and safer to treat heart injuries soon after a heart attack. It also showed promise for treating other hard-to-reach injuries like brain trauma and lung problems, opening up new possibilities in medicine.

**What's next step** – The researchers plan to ask for permission to start testing the biomaterial in human patients to make sure it is safe and effective. If successful, it could become a new treatment option delivered through existing medical procedures like IVs or angioplasty.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists created an injectable material that travels through the bloodstream to repair damaged heart tissue, offering a less invasive way to help the heart heal after a heart attack.
articles.personality_title_fr: Nouveau matériau injectable aide à réparer les tissus cardiaques endommagés de l’intérieur
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les crises cardiaques endommagent gravement le muscle cardiaque, et les traitements actuels restaurent le flux sanguin sans pouvoir réparer les tissus blessés. Les scientifiques cherchent des moyens pour aider le cœur à guérir et réduire l'inflammation.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Californie à San Diego ont créé un nouveau biomatériau injectable qui circule dans le sang jusqu’aux tissus cardiaques abîmés. Testé chez des rats et des cochons, ce matériau se fixe aux vaisseaux endommagés, aide à fermer les brèches, réduit l’inflammation et favorise la réparation. Contrairement aux traitements précédents, il peut être administré par perfusion ou lors de procédures cardiaques courantes.

**Impact** – Cette méthode est moins invasive et peut se répartir uniformément dans les tissus blessés, facilitant un traitement rapide et sûr après une crise cardiaque. Elle montre aussi un potentiel pour soigner d’autres blessures difficiles d’accès comme les traumatismes cérébraux ou les problèmes pulmonaires.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent obtenir l’autorisation pour tester ce biomatériau chez des patients humains afin d’en vérifier la sécurité et l’efficacité. En cas de succès, il pourrait devenir un nouveau traitement administré via des procédures médicales existantes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont mis au point un matériau injectable qui circule dans le sang pour réparer les tissus cardiaques endommagés, offrant un moyen moins invasif d’aider le cœur à guérir après une crise cardiaque.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo material injectable ayuda a reparar tejido cardíaco dañado desde el interior del cuerpo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los ataques al corazón dañan gravemente el músculo cardíaco, y los tratamientos actuales se enfocan en restaurar el flujo sanguíneo, pero no pueden reparar el tejido lesionado. Los científicos buscan formas de ayudar al corazón a sanar y reducir la inflamación.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de California en San Diego desarrollaron un nuevo biomaterial injectable que viaja por el torrente sanguíneo hasta el tejido cardíaco dañado. Probado en ratas y cerdos, este material se adhiere a los vasos sanguíneos lesionados, ayuda a cerrar las brechas, reduce la inflamación y fomenta la reparación del tejido. A diferencia de tratamientos anteriores, puede administrarse por vía intravenosa o durante procedimientos cardíacos comunes.

**Impacto** – Este método es menos invasivo y puede distribuirse de manera uniforme en el tejido dañado, facilitando un tratamiento rápido y seguro después de un ataque al corazón. También mostró potencial para tratar otras lesiones difíciles de alcanzar, como traumatismos cerebrales y problemas pulmonares.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean solicitar permiso para comenzar pruebas en humanos para asegurar que sea seguro y efectivo. Si tiene éxito, podría convertirse en una nueva opción de tratamiento administrada mediante procedimientos médicos existentes.

**Frase clave** – Científicos crearon un material injectable que viaja por el torrente sanguíneo para reparar tejido cardíaco dañado, ofreciendo una forma menos invasiva de ayudar al corazón a sanar tras un ataque al corazón.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a glowing, flowing network of blood vessels gently infused with tiny, radiant nano-particles that softly bind and mend delicate gaps in the vessel walls, set against a warm, abstract background of healing heart tissue subtly textured like a supportive scaffold, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones and soft reds to evoke regeneration and calm restoration.

articles.title: Scientists boost strawberry flavor and nutrition without changing growth
articles.summary: A surprising genetic twist shows that boosting a seemingly ordinary “housekeeping” gene can dramatically improve fruit quality without any trade-offs. By increasing the activity of a tRNA-related gene in strawberries, researchers unlocked richer color, stronger aroma, and higher levels of health-boosting compounds like anthocyanins and terpenoids. Even more striking, these enhancements came with zero impact on plant growth, fruit size, or sweetness—avoiding the usual downsides of metabolic tinkering.
articles.content:
Improving the quality of fruit while preserving normal plant growth has long been a difficult goal in agriculture. New research suggests this balance may be easier to achieve than previously thought. Scientists found that increasing the activity of a conserved "housekeeping" gene can enhance both the nutritional value and sensory appeal of fruit. By boosting the expression of a gene linked to tRNA, the team increased levels of anthocyanins and terpenoids, compounds that contribute to color, aroma, and antioxidant properties. These improvements occurred without any measurable changes in plant development, fruit size, or sugar levels. The results point to an unexpected role for genes typically associated with basic cellular maintenance, suggesting they can also influence key metabolic traits in fruit.

Compounds such as anthocyanins and terpenoids are essential for fruit color, flavor, aroma, and overall nutritional value. However, attempts to increase these compounds often create unwanted side effects. This is because their production is closely tied to plant hormones. Cytokinins, for example, regulate both plant growth and secondary metabolism, so altering their levels can change plant structure and growth patterns.

A lesser-known group of cytokinin-related genes called tRNA-type isopentenyl transferases has received little attention. These genes are generally considered to serve routine cellular functions rather than actively regulate plant traits. Whether they could improve fruit quality without interfering with growth was not well understood, making them an intriguing target for further study.

A Hidden Gene With Big Effects

Researchers from Nanjing Agricultural University and the University of Connecticut, publishing in Horticulture Research, explored this possibility using woodland strawberries. They focused on a housekeeping gene called FveIPT2. By engineering plants to produce higher levels of this gene, they observed a clear improvement in fruit quality.

The modified plants produced significantly more anthocyanins and terpenoids in ripe fruit, yet showed no differences in growth, fruit size, or sugar content compared to normal plants. This finding challenges the long-held view that housekeeping genes play only passive roles and highlights their potential in crop improvement.

No Impact on Growth or Development

FveIPT2 is involved in tRNA modification and is linked to the production of cis-zeatin, a type of cytokinin. Unlike other cytokinin-related genes that strongly influence plant growth, increasing FveIPT2 activity caused only minor changes in overall cytokinin levels. The plants developed normally, with no visible abnormalities. They flowered and produced fruit as expected, and there were no changes in fruit weight, shape, or sweetness.

Richer Color, Stronger Aroma, Better Nutrition

Even though plant growth remained stable, the chemical makeup of the fruit changed substantially. Levels of anthocyanins, flavonoids, and phenolic compounds all increased, giving the fruit a deeper red color. Detailed analysis showed significant rises in nine specific anthocyanins, including compounds derived from cyanidin and pelargonidin that are known for their antioxidant properties.

At the same time, nearly half of the detected terpenoids increased. These included monoterpenoids, sesquiterpenoids, and triterpenoids, which play important roles in aroma and flavor.

Improved Flavor Profile

The changes were not limited to color and nutrition. Aromatic compounds associated with pleasant floral notes, such as linalool, became more abundant. Meanwhile, compounds linked to harsher, resin-like odors decreased. Gene expression studies confirmed that key pathways responsible for producing and transporting these compounds were more active.

Together, the findings show that FveIPT2 can selectively enhance fruit chemistry without triggering the typical hormone-driven changes that affect growth.

Rethinking "Housekeeping" Genes

"This study shows that genes we usually think of as 'housekeeping' can have surprisingly specific and valuable effects," the researchers noted. "By targeting a tRNA-type gene rather than classical hormone regulators, we were able to improve fruit color, aroma, and nutritional compounds without the growth penalties that often accompany metabolic engineering. These findings suggest that basic cellular pathways may quietly shape fruit quality, offering breeders new tools that are both effective and biologically gentle."

A New Path for Crop Improvement

The results position FveIPT2 as a promising target for improving fruit quality in strawberries and possibly other crops. Because this approach boosts beneficial pigments and aroma compounds without reducing yield or plant vigor, it could be especially useful for developing high-quality produce.

More broadly, the research challenges the idea that housekeeping genes are only involved in routine cellular processes. By revealing their influence on secondary metabolism, the study points to new strategies for enhancing crops in ways that maintain both productivity and quality.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in agricultural biotechnology where modifying a housekeeping gene in strawberries enhances fruit flavor, color, aroma, and nutritional value without compromising plant growth or yield. This advancement has broad implications for crop improvement, food quality, and human health by increasing antioxidant compounds in widely consumed fruits. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a novel approach with potential large-scale benefits for agriculture and nutrition.
articles.personality_title: Scientists improve strawberry flavor and nutrition without affecting growth
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**
Improving the taste and health benefits of fruit often causes plants to grow less well or produce smaller fruit. This has made it hard for farmers and scientists to make better fruit without losing important qualities like size or sweetness.

**What happened**
Scientists from Nanjing Agricultural University and the University of Connecticut studied a gene called FveIPT2 in woodland strawberries. This gene is usually involved in basic cell functions. By increasing its activity, they found the strawberries became deeper red, smelled better, and had more healthy compounds called anthocyanins and terpenoids. Importantly, the plants grew normally, and the fruit size and sweetness stayed the same.

**Impact**
This discovery shows that a gene thought to only help with simple cell tasks can actually improve fruit quality without hurting plant growth. Unlike other genes that change plant hormones and cause side effects, FveIPT2 boosts flavor and nutrition quietly. This could help farmers grow tastier, healthier strawberries without losing yield.

**What's next step**
Researchers hope to use this gene to improve other crops too. They want to explore if similar housekeeping genes can enhance fruit quality in different plants while keeping them strong and productive. This could lead to better fruits available to consumers without extra farming problems.

**One-sentence takeaway**
Boosting a basic cell gene in strawberries improves fruit color, aroma, and nutrition without affecting plant growth or sweetness.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques améliorent la saveur et la nutrition des fraises sans affecter leur croissance
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**
Améliorer le goût et les bienfaits pour la santé des fruits entraîne souvent une baisse de la croissance des plantes ou de la taille des fruits. Cela complique la tâche des agriculteurs et des scientifiques pour obtenir de meilleurs fruits sans perdre des qualités importantes comme la taille ou la douceur.

**Ce qui s'est passé**
Des chercheurs de l'Université agricole de Nanjing et de l'Université du Connecticut ont étudié un gène appelé FveIPT2 chez la fraise des bois. Ce gène est normalement impliqué dans des fonctions cellulaires simples. En augmentant son activité, ils ont constaté que les fraises devenaient plus rouges, avaient un meilleur parfum et contenaient plus de composés bénéfiques appelés anthocyanines et terpènes. De plus, les plantes poussaient normalement, et la taille et la douceur des fruits restaient identiques.

**Impact**
Cette découverte montre qu'un gène considéré comme aidant uniquement aux tâches cellulaires simples peut en réalité améliorer la qualité des fruits sans nuire à la croissance des plantes. Contrairement à d'autres gènes qui modifient les hormones végétales et provoquent des effets secondaires, FveIPT2 améliore discrètement la saveur et la nutrition. Cela pourrait aider les agriculteurs à cultiver des fraises plus savoureuses et plus saines sans perdre en rendement.

**Prochaine étape**
Les chercheurs espèrent utiliser ce gène pour améliorer d'autres cultures. Ils veulent étudier si des gènes similaires dits « de ménage » peuvent améliorer la qualité des fruits dans différentes plantes tout en les gardant fortes et productives. Cela pourrait permettre d'avoir de meilleurs fruits disponibles pour les consommateurs sans problèmes agricoles supplémentaires.

**Phrase clé**
Augmenter un gène de base cellulaire chez la fraise améliore la couleur, l'arôme et la nutrition du fruit sans affecter la croissance ou la douceur.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos mejoran el sabor y la nutrición de las fresas sin afectar su crecimiento
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**
Mejorar el sabor y los beneficios para la salud de las frutas suele hacer que las plantas crezcan menos o que los frutos sean más pequeños. Esto dificulta que agricultores y científicos obtengan frutas mejores sin perder cualidades importantes como tamaño o dulzura.

**Qué pasó**
Científicos de la Universidad Agrícola de Nanjing y la Universidad de Connecticut estudiaron un gen llamado FveIPT2 en fresas silvestres. Este gen normalmente ayuda en funciones celulares básicas. Al aumentar su actividad, descubrieron que las fresas se volvieron más rojas, olían mejor y tenían más compuestos saludables llamados antocianinas y terpenoides. Lo importante es que las plantas crecieron normalmente y el tamaño y dulzura del fruto no cambiaron.

**Impacto**
Este hallazgo muestra que un gen considerado solo para tareas celulares simples puede mejorar la calidad del fruto sin afectar el crecimiento de la planta. A diferencia de otros genes que alteran las hormonas y causan efectos secundarios, FveIPT2 mejora el sabor y la nutrición de forma discreta. Esto podría ayudar a los agricultores a cultivar fresas más sabrosas y saludables sin perder producción.

**Próximo paso**
Los investigadores esperan usar este gen para mejorar otros cultivos. Quieren ver si genes similares pueden aumentar la calidad del fruto en diferentes plantas manteniéndolas fuertes y productivas. Esto podría llevar a que haya mejores frutas disponibles para los consumidores sin problemas extra para los agricultores.

**Frase clave**
Aumentar un gen básico en las células de la fresa mejora el color, aroma y nutrición del fruto sin afectar el crecimiento o la dulzura.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of vibrant woodland strawberries glowing with deep red hues and surrounded by delicate swirling patterns representing anthocyanins and terpenoids, set against a softly lit background symbolizing balanced growth with subtle, abstract cellular structures hinting at the hidden gene’s nurturing influence.

articles.title: Quantum ‘thermometer’ takes temperatures inside living cancer cells
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 05 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01444-5Nanosensors showed that different parts of the cell varied by as much as 1 ºC.
articles.content:
A cancer cell (nucleus in blue, cellular ‘skeleton’ in green). Scientists have devised nanosensors that can measure the temperature inside cancer cells, including the cell nuclei.Credit: Howard Vindin, The University of Sydney/Science Photo Library

To check your temperature, doctors place a thermometer under your tongue. Researchers have now created a thermometer small enough to check the temperature of a single living cell, and even individual cellular regions, such as the nucleus. Once refined, similar technologies could help scientists to study metabolism and other chemical reactions of life on the smallest scale. The work was reported last Wednesday in Science Advances1.

Why ‘quantum proteins’ could be the next big thing in biology

Before now, the best cell thermometers were nanodiamonds with defects known as nitrogen-vacancy centres. But each diamond is slightly different, meaning that readings are not consistent across sensors.

The authors of the new paper created an alternative: molecular quantum nanosensors. The team embedded molecules of a hydrocarbon compound called pentacene into a crystal. Then they tumbled the crystal to break it into smaller particles, coating them in a polymer during this process to prevent clumping and maintain their safety to cells. The final sensors were either 200 or 500 nanometers across, a small fraction of the diameter of a human red blood cell.

These sensors are considered quantum because their behaviour depends on ‘superpositions’ between particular quantum states of the pentacene molecules’ electrons — that is, the electrons are occupying multiple states at once.

Green light, red light

Shining green lasers at the sensors made them glow red. But simultaneously stimulating the sensors with microwaves at certain frequencies slightly dimmed their glow. The temperature of the sensors’ surroundings (and, by extension, the sensors themselves) affects the dim-inducing frequency. The authors worked out the precise relationship between the frequency that triggered dimming and temperature, allowing them to infer one from the other.

Quantum correlations enhance the sensitivity of room-temperature sensors

To get the sensors inside cells, the scientists either bathed cancer cells in solutions containing the sensors until they were absorbed or injected them into the nuclei. The team then applied their laser-and-microwave system to the sensors and noted which frequencies of microwaves caused them to dim, an index of cellular temperature.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough: the development of quantum nanosensors capable of measuring temperature inside living cancer cells at a subcellular level. This advancement has broad implications for understanding cellular metabolism and chemical reactions, potentially improving cancer research and treatment. The story focuses on a single, well-explained innovation with clear real-world impact on health science.
articles.personality_title: New quantum nanosensors measure temperature inside living cancer cells
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long wanted to measure temperature inside living cells to better understand how cells work. Until now, tools to do this were large or gave inconsistent results.

**What happened** – Researchers developed tiny sensors called quantum nanosensors that can measure temperature inside cancer cells, even inside parts like the nucleus. These sensors are made from a special crystal containing pentacene molecules. When green light shines on them, they glow red, but microwaves at certain frequencies make the glow dim. The frequency that causes dimming changes with temperature, letting scientists find out how hot or cold the cell or its parts are.

**Impact** – This is the first time temperature can be measured so precisely inside live cells without harming them. Knowing the temperature in different parts of a cell helps scientists understand cell processes like metabolism and chemical reactions better. This could lead to new ways to study diseases like cancer and improve treatments.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue improving these nanosensors and testing them in different types of cells. They hope to use this technology to watch how cell temperatures change during disease or treatment, which might help doctors develop better medicines.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Tiny quantum sensors now let scientists measure temperature inside living cancer cells, opening new paths for understanding and treating diseases.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouveaux nanosenseurs quantiques mesurent la température à l'intérieur des cellules cancéreuses vivantes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent depuis longtemps à mesurer la température à l'intérieur des cellules vivantes pour mieux comprendre leur fonctionnement. Jusqu'à présent, les outils disponibles étaient trop grands ou donnaient des résultats peu fiables.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs ont développé de minuscules capteurs appelés nanosenseurs quantiques capables de mesurer la température à l'intérieur des cellules cancéreuses, même dans des parties comme le noyau. Ces capteurs sont fabriqués à partir d'un cristal spécial contenant des molécules de pentacène. Lorsqu'on les éclaire avec une lumière verte, ils brillent en rouge, mais des micro-ondes à certaines fréquences font diminuer cette lumière. La fréquence qui provoque cette baisse change selon la température, ce qui permet de connaître la chaleur dans la cellule ou ses parties.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que la température peut être mesurée avec autant de précision à l'intérieur de cellules vivantes sans les abîmer. Connaître la température dans différentes parties d'une cellule aide les scientifiques à mieux comprendre des processus comme le métabolisme et les réactions chimiques. Cela pourrait ouvrir la voie à de nouvelles études sur des maladies comme le cancer et améliorer les traitements.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à perfectionner ces nanosenseurs et à les tester dans différents types de cellules. Ils espèrent utiliser cette technologie pour observer comment la température des cellules change pendant une maladie ou un traitement, ce qui pourrait aider à développer de meilleurs médicaments.

**Résumé en une phrase** – De minuscules nanosenseurs quantiques permettent désormais de mesurer la température à l’intérieur des cellules cancéreuses vivantes, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour comprendre et traiter les maladies.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevos nanosensores cuánticos miden la temperatura dentro de células cancerosas vivas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han querido medir la temperatura dentro de las células vivas para entender mejor cómo funcionan. Hasta ahora, las herramientas eran muy grandes o daban resultados inconsistentes.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores desarrollaron nanosensores cuánticos muy pequeños que pueden medir la temperatura dentro de células cancerosas, incluso en partes como el núcleo. Estos sensores están hechos de un cristal especial con moléculas de pentaceno. Cuando se iluminan con luz verde, brillan en rojo, pero las microondas a ciertas frecuencias hacen que el brillo disminuya. La frecuencia que causa esta disminución cambia con la temperatura, lo que permite saber qué tan caliente o fría está la célula o sus partes.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que se puede medir la temperatura con tanta precisión dentro de células vivas sin dañarlas. Saber la temperatura en diferentes partes de la célula ayuda a los científicos a entender mejor procesos como el metabolismo y las reacciones químicas. Esto podría ayudar a estudiar enfermedades como el cáncer y mejorar tratamientos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores continuarán mejorando estos nanosensores y probándolos en distintos tipos de células. Esperan usar esta tecnología para observar cómo cambia la temperatura celular durante una enfermedad o tratamiento, lo que podría ayudar a crear mejores medicinas.

**Resumen en una frase** – Pequeños sensores cuánticos permiten ahora medir la temperatura dentro de células cancerosas vivas, abriendo nuevas formas de entender y tratar enfermedades.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing blue cell nucleus surrounded by a delicate green cellular skeleton, with tiny luminous red quantum nanosensors embedded inside, softly pulsing under gentle beams of green light and subtle microwave waves visualized as faint ripples around the sensors.

articles.title: Woman in cancer remission without treatment in highly unusual case
articles.summary: A biopsy of a woman's cancer seems to have triggered an immune response against the tumour, putting her into remission
articles.content:
A biopsy of a woman's cancer seems to have triggered an immune response against the tumour, putting her into remission

An X-ray of the woman’s arm, with the arrow pointing to her tumour before the biopsy Gannon M C, Gabor R M, Gupta A, et al. (April 15, 2026)

A woman who had a cancerous tumour in her arm is now in remission despite receiving no treatment. This is thought to be due to a biopsy she had to diagnose the growth causing an immune response against the cancer.

She is one of just nine known cases where a biopsy led to a cancer of this type, which affected her connective tissue, disappearing within a few weeks.

“It’s extremely remarkable,” says Toby Lawrence at the Centre for Immunology of Marseille-Luminy in France, who wasn’t involved in the case. “It really suggests some kind of immune activation in response to the injury of the biopsy, because it had extremely rapid effects on tumour growth.”

The woman, aged 59, noticed the rapidly growing lump a few weeks before she sought help, at which point it was 2 centimetres wide. “It had been progressing rapidly, causing some pain and discomfort; she was concerned,” says Rohit Sharma at Marshfield Clinic Health System in Wisconsin, who treated the woman.

Sharma and his colleagues marked the tumour’s location with tattoo ink and took a biopsy by briefly inserting a thin needle into it. This revealed that the lump, which was located in the connective tissue between the woman’s skin and muscle, was a tumour called myxofibrosarcoma that contained aggressive cancer cells. “It was likely to spread, [and] cancers kill by spreading,” says Sharma.

Free newsletter Sign up to Eight Weeks to a Healthier You Your science-backed guide to the easy habits that will help you sleep well, stress less, eat smarter and age better. Sign up to newsletter

Two weeks later, the woman returned for surgery to remove the tumour, but the clinicians were surprised to discover it had completely disappeared. “She said that after the biopsy, it had started to go down within three [to] four days,” says Sharma.

To make sure the tumour really had gone, the team surgically excised the tissue surrounding where it had been, which confirmed there were no cancer cells. “The timing of the biopsy and resolution suggests there’s an immune reaction occurring,” says Sharma. Any type of cancer disappearing after biopsy is highly unusual, but this has been most commonly reported for cancers that are more easily recognised by the immune system, such as those of the skin, he says.

Taking a biopsy may cause some cancer cells to die and release inflammatory signals that activate first-responder immune cells, such as natural killer cells, to kill off damaged tumour tissue within hours to days, says Sharma. This may then cause even more proteins that are specifically in or on cancer cells to be spewed out, says Sharma. In turn, T cells of the immune system probably also become activated to recognise and destroy cancer cells, he says.

But, of course, this doesn’t occur – at least in a meaningful way – in most people, so the lucky few probably have certain genetic factors and environmental exposures that mean their immune systems can respond in this way, says Lawrence.

A magnetic resonance imaging scan of the tumour Gannon M C, Gabor R M, Gupta A, et al. (April 15, 2026)

Unpicking what those are, such as by analysing the genomes and medical histories of these rare cases, could reveal ways to improve general responses to cancer therapy, says Lawrence.

Analysing how the immune system of mice with cancer responds to minor tissue injuries could also help to reveal what’s going on, says Caetano Reis e Sousa at the Francis Crick Institute in London. “If we understand the mechanism by which biopsy is making those cells visible to the immune system, we could harness it and develop drugs that do that too,” he says.

Sharma says the team hopes to explore this in the next few years, after compiling a database of similar cases.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a rare and inspiring medical case where a cancer biopsy triggered an immune response leading to remission without treatment. This discovery has significant implications for cancer research and therapy development, potentially benefiting many patients in the future. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a meaningful breakthrough in understanding immune activation against cancer.
articles.personality_title: Cancer tumor disappears after biopsy triggers immune response
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – A 59-year-old woman had a fast-growing cancerous lump in her arm, diagnosed as myxofibrosarcoma, a type of aggressive cancer in the connective tissue. Normally, such tumors require treatment like surgery or chemotherapy.

**What happened** – Doctors took a biopsy of the tumor by inserting a thin needle to collect a sample. Unexpectedly, within a few days after the biopsy, the tumor began shrinking and completely disappeared by the time she returned for surgery two weeks later. Tests confirmed no cancer cells remained.

**Impact** – This case is very rare; only nine known patients have had similar results where a biopsy alone caused an immune system attack that cleared the cancer. The biopsy likely caused the immune system to recognize and destroy the tumor quickly. Understanding how this happens could help develop new treatments that activate the immune system against cancer without harsh therapies.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to study more cases like this to find genetic or environmental reasons why some people’s immune systems respond this way. They also want to use animal studies to learn how small injuries like biopsies help the immune system spot cancer cells. This knowledge could lead to new drugs that mimic this effect.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A biopsy triggered a natural immune response that made a dangerous cancer tumor disappear without treatment, opening new paths for cancer research and therapy.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une tumeur cancéreuse disparaît après une biopsie qui déclenche la réponse immunitaire
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Une femme de 59 ans avait une grosseur cancéreuse qui grandissait rapidement dans son bras, un myxofibrosarcome, un cancer agressif des tissus conjonctifs. Habituellement, ce type de tumeur nécessite des traitements comme la chirurgie ou la chimiothérapie.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Les médecins ont fait une biopsie en insérant une fine aiguille pour prélever un échantillon. De façon inattendue, quelques jours après la biopsie, la tumeur a commencé à rétrécir et avait complètement disparu quand elle est revenue pour la chirurgie deux semaines plus tard. Les tests ont confirmé qu’il n’y avait plus de cellules cancéreuses.

**Impact** – Ce cas est très rare ; seulement neuf patients connus ont eu un résultat similaire où la biopsie seule a déclenché une attaque du système immunitaire qui a éliminé le cancer. La biopsie a probablement aidé le système immunitaire à reconnaître et détruire rapidement la tumeur. Comprendre ce mécanisme pourrait aider à créer de nouveaux traitements qui activent le système immunitaire sans les effets secondaires des thérapies lourdes.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent étudier plus de cas similaires pour découvrir les raisons génétiques ou environnementales qui expliquent cette réponse immunitaire. Ils souhaitent aussi utiliser des études sur des animaux pour comprendre comment des petites blessures comme les biopsies aident le système immunitaire à détecter les cellules cancéreuses. Ces connaissances pourraient conduire à de nouveaux médicaments qui reproduisent cet effet.

**En une phrase** – Une biopsie a déclenché une réponse immunitaire naturelle qui a fait disparaître une tumeur cancéreuse dangereuse sans traitement, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour la recherche et la thérapie du cancer.
articles.personality_title_es: Un tumor canceroso desaparece tras una biopsia que activa la respuesta inmune
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Una mujer de 59 años tenía un bulto canceroso que crecía rápido en su brazo, un mixofibrosarcoma, un tipo de cáncer agresivo en el tejido conectivo. Normalmente, este tipo de tumor requiere tratamientos como cirugía o quimioterapia.

**Qué ocurrió** – Los médicos hicieron una biopsia insertando una aguja fina para tomar una muestra. Inesperadamente, pocos días después de la biopsia, el tumor comenzó a reducirse y desapareció por completo cuando volvió para la cirugía dos semanas después. Las pruebas confirmaron que no quedaban células cancerosas.

**Impacto** – Este caso es muy raro; solo se conocen nueve pacientes con resultados similares donde solo la biopsia provocó que el sistema inmune atacara y eliminara el cáncer. Es probable que la biopsia ayudara al sistema inmunitario a reconocer y destruir el tumor rápidamente. Entender cómo sucede esto puede ayudar a crear nuevos tratamientos que activen el sistema inmune sin terapias agresivas.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean estudiar más casos así para encontrar razones genéticas o ambientales que expliquen por qué algunas personas responden así. También usarán estudios en animales para aprender cómo pequeñas lesiones como biopsias ayudan al sistema inmune a detectar células cancerosas. Esto podría llevar a nuevos medicamentos que imiten este efecto.

**Frase clave** – Una biopsia activó una respuesta inmune natural que hizo desaparecer un tumor canceroso peligroso sin tratamiento, abriendo nuevas posibilidades para la investigación y tratamiento del cáncer.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a delicate arm silhouette with a glowing, softly pulsing cluster of cells at the biopsy site, surrounded by gentle, abstract representations of vigilant immune cells—depicted as protective, radiant orbs or shields—engaged in a harmonious, dynamic interaction symbolizing the immune system’s unexpected activation and tumor remission.
